content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 17,
1992
Robert E.
Leidigh
OLSON,
CONNELLY,
HAGEL
FONG
&
LEIDIGH
Capitol Bank Center
300 Capitol Mall,
Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-424
Dear Mr.
Leidigh:
This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
and confirms the telephone advice provided to you on August 5,
1992,
concerning the disclosure obligations of City of El Cajon
Councilrnember Walter E.
“Bob” McClellan under the provisions of
the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Mr.
Walter E.
“Bob” McClellan was elected to the City Council
of the City of El Cajon and assumed office on July 7,
1992.
He
has also been appointed as the city’s representative on several
San Diego area xnulti-jurisdictional agencies.
He was required to
file his Assuming Office Statement of Economic Interests for these
various positions on or before August 6,
1992.
Mr.
McClellan owns more than 50 percent and is the majority
shareholder of McClellan Buick,
Inc.,
a new and used car
dealership located in La Mesa,
a nearby city to his home
jurisdiction of El Cajon.
He receives a salary and other benefits
and perquisites from McClellan Buick,
Inc.
McClellan Buick,
Inc.
sells cars to residents of his jurisdiction.
Thus,
Mr.
McClellan
will disclose both his investment interest and his
income from
McClellan Buick,
Inc.,
as well as many customers who are sources
of income to him,
in his Assuming Office Statement of Economic
Interests.
You seek our advice to determine whether Mr.
McClellan
•
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory refetences
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated;
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807
S
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-424
Page
2
is required to disclose receipt of the following economic
benefits.
question
1
As part of a Buick Motor Division national promotion,
McClellan Buick,
Inc. purchased a high volume of advertising in a
local radio station.
The station provided a trip to Ireland to
McClellan Buick,
Inc.
as a volume discount or rebate.
Mr. Mcclellan participated in this trip to Ireland.
You have stated in your request for advice that Mr.
McClellan
believes this was provided in the normal course of business.
It
is your belief that it was provided without regard to his official
status because he held no public office at the time.
Moreover, it
was not provided to him individually,
but rather to McClellan
Buick,
Inc.
We advised you that the Commision is in the process of
adopting regulations which may alter our analysis of the
reportability of gifts of travel.
However,
we are presently
advising that travel which is provided to a bona fide business
organization is income to the business and not a gift to the
official who is a majority shareholder of the business.
Therefore,
Mr. McClellan would not be subject to the $1,000 gift
and honoraria limits of Section 89501 if he benefits from such
travel.
Accordingly, the trip to Ireland would be considered income
to McClellan Buick, Inc.,
so long as it was offered to other
advertisers who did similar volumes of advertising.
In that
event, the trip would be disciosable on Schedule H-2 of
Mr. McClellan’s Assuming Office Statement if Mr. McClellan’s pro
rata share of the value of the trip equalled $10,000 or more.
Question
2.
In two separate instances, McClellan Buick,
Inc. participated
in competitive contests sponsored by Buick Motor Division.
McClellan Buick,
Inc.
was a winner in those competitions.
In one
contest, the prize was a trip to Hawaii.
In the other contest, it
was a trip to a seminar in Washington,
D.C.
In one instance,
an
entry fee also had to be paid in order for the dealership to
participate in the competition.
The prizes were won by McClellan
Buick,
Inc.
and Mr. McClellan went on the trips.
We advised you that the trips to Hawaii and to Washington
D.C.
were bona fide contest prizes and would be treated as income
to McClellan Buick,
Inc.
Accordingly,
they would be reportable if
Mr. McClellan’s pro rata share of the value of each..trip equalled
$10,000 or more.


Our File No.
A—92--424
Page
3
question
3
Mr.
McClellan also used tickets to various sporting and
entertainment events such as baseball and football games which
were provided to McClellan Buick,
Inc.
by local radio stations as
goodwill or promotional gestures because McClellan Buick,
Inc.
advertises on the stations.
These tickets were not related to any
specific volume of business but were provided by the stations from
time to time to various customers.
Mr. McClellan did not hold
public office at the time when he accepted these tickets.
You were advised that the tickets to entertainment events
would also be treated as income to McClellan Buick, Inc.,
and
would be reportable on Schedule 11-2 of Mr. McClellan’s Assuming
Office Statement if his pro rata share of all income to Mcclellan
Buick,
Inc.
was $10,000 or more,
so long as the tickets were
provided to McClellan Buick, Inc.,
as part of the promotional
efforts of radio stations in the normal course of business.
Mr. Mcclellan must make diligent efforts to ascertain the value of
the tickets to determine whether the reporting threshold has been
reached.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Blanca M.
Breeze
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:BMB:aa
